item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

Holy heck, this is linked everywhere. It's pretty much the "Temporal Anomalies Apartment" in its entirety. I wen through the backlinks, and I found some SCPs that I can hopefully link back to this one.

Editing this:

Dr. ████████ █████.

To this:

Dr. ████████ █████.

Editing this:

When read from the beginning, the rear of SCP-1780-3 reportedly describes in detail the nature of ‘tachyon flux’ [DOCUMENTATION INFOHAZARD EXPUNGED]

To this:

When read from the beginning, the rear of SCP-1780-3 reportedly describes in detail the nature of ‘tachyon flux’ [DOCUMENTATION INFOHAZARD EXPUNGED]

Editing this:

█████ (█) SCP objects, including [DATA EXPUNGED]

To this:

█████ (█) SCP objects, including [DATA EXPUNGED]

EDIT: I just remembered SCP-3780 exists and deserves to be linked here

Editing this:

You’re welcome, but that’s the last warning.

To this:

You’re welcome, but that’s the last warning.
Congrats! It's very well deserved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm reading this as some random dude keeping agents from various timelines in his looping office, and just trapping them there under the pretense of danger, when there actually isn't any they can actually prevent this way. either way +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it's Schrödinger's Foundation, until someone opens the "box"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just fantastic.

Time travel is far from the most original premise, but your execution is perfect.

+1 from the future

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
FYE Only, related to SCP-1870 documentation.
This stands for SPOILERS.

It takes place (meta)after Thad retires, and wipes himself and his department out of causality as well as he is able.

I KNEW IT

Ahem. Sorry.

So, essentially, Delta-Tee fulfilled the sixth item in Document 1780-WL, or Thad decided that the whole thing wasn't worth it, created far too many of the retrocausal disturbances they were supposed to solve (thus, causing them in the first place), or simply wanted to have some sanity in his last years.

Also, the concept of esoteric when compared to anomalous is new to me. Has this something to do with -EX articles? (Haven't read any of those yet.) However, when you know that something has/is already been done/done (my head hurts), it does make sense to have it done, or having it done, or making it happen, or balls you know what I mean

Also:

I just think of a string of syllables that seems, to me, to embody some aspect of the character, and then I try and fit that into names that actually exist where possible.

Huh. Makes sense. : O

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
how do you come up with such badass names for your characters?

That's not really a question I can answer in a way that's satisfying. I just think of a string of syllables that seems, to me, to embody some aspect of the character, and then I try and fit that into names that actually exist where possible. I have a very audio-centric memory and imagination, so it's not as challenging for me as I imagine it is for others.

Is it possible that the Xyank Prime Foundation (XF?) exists in a universe where what "canon" Foundation considers anomalous math/physics/memetics/SCIENCE/people is regarded as normal, non-anomalous or commonplace enough as to deem it impossible to contain?

Yes and no? The Foundation that Xyank is from allows him to operate because they already know that he exits in their records far into the past (he published a paper on how to build a stable time-bubble in 1892). They're in a position where they can either keep him locked up and risk creating a paradox, or trust his research and allow him to continue operating in such a way that it closes these causal loops. Thad also has a more strict definition of "anomalous" than the Foundation at large. To his mind, if you can reverse engineer a thing, find the mechanism for it, and reproduce it in a laboratory, it's no longer anomalous; just esoteric.

My original idea for this article?

Maybe a spoiler?
Yeah, kinda a spoiler...

It takes place (meta)after Thad retires, and wipes himself and his department out of causality as well as he is able. But all of those other timelines still exist from beginning to end, in parallel with this one, which is now considered "baseline" by the people who inhabit it. Unfortunately, he also knew, from seeing versions of the Foundation where he was considered an anomaly that never existed, that he had to leave a door open. So he did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
FortuneFavorsTheBold, question.

Is it possible that the Xyank Prime Foundation (XF?) exists in a universe where what "canon" Foundation considers anomalous math/physics/memetics/SCIENCE/people is regarded as normal, non-anomalous or commonplace enough as to deem it impossible to contain?

Also, how do you come up with such badass names for your characters?

Answer the second question first.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
What's even better is that this article has no downvotes.
No I'm not jealous at all

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how you called the RCTF Δt. Δ means "change in" and "t" is often used as the variable for time; so the RCTF's name is literally "Change in time."

Applause

Three cheers for Fortune!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this article so much. It's complex, layered and rife for story telling potential. Upvoted in a heartbeat. I do have a couple of minor quibbles though.

1) It seems strange to me that the name plate is considered the SCP. The nameplate is merely a key to a door, a door which the foundation already knows there are many, many more uncontained keys to. I would think that the Temporal Anomalies Department itself or at least the office would be considered the SCP, and that it would probably be Euclid or even Keter since it's unpredictable and essentially uncontainable (and has kidnapped and killed several Foundation personal). Making the nameplate the SCP and classifying it as Safe seems to me to a head in the sand approach by a Foundation trying very hard not to acknowledge that it's powerless to stop a powerful anomaly by pretending something else that's easy to contain is the real anomaly.

2) How do Task Force RCTF-Δt Agents travel through time? SCP-1780-3 and the way people instantly vanish upon finishing reading (and presumably, understanding) the information (and test exercises!) about temporal displacement on the back of it seem to imply that they are somehow willing themselves through time just by understanding how time really works, or something like that. But if that's the case, why would they need to self-terminate when captured?

3) I'm surprised by how the article avoids directly addressing the whole "alternate universe Foundation time police" thing, or the fact that Foundation agents have actually defected to join them. I would think that a lot of Foundation personal would be sympathetic to the idea of stopping major catastrophes before they even happen, to the point that the Foundation would probably feel the need to try and discredit the Temporal Anomalies Department. Maybe there could be something like a message from O-5 at the end of the article? Something like: Remember people, this supposed alternate Foundation cannot be trusted, they have never offered any direct proof to the Foundation to verify their claims, and that even if they are telling the truth, they are wrong. The Foundation does not stop temporal anomalies by further mucking up the timeline, we do not use SCPs to contain other SCPs, and there is no catastrophe worth creating a paradox and destroying the entire universe in order to stop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alternate Foundation(s):
Filing paperwork for your camera equipment is wasting my time and your resources.
You will not recover them, please stop trying.

Ha! That is great! Love the whole thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Incorporate 699 somehow please?

item.title: Re: Image Policy
item.content:encoded: Oh, and that reminds me — you should try and take the picture again. It's blurry.

item.title: Image Policy
item.content:encoded: Per the new image policy, I'd just like to note that this is my own image, owned by me, and released under CC/BY/SA/3.0 along with the rest of the work. All images on the attached Document 1780-WL are likewise my own creations, and subject to the same license.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…BRILLIANT!
Take my upvotes! Take them all!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Analysis of Office CB-████ has yielded no evidence of anomalous activity and may be safely inhabited by Fred.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gotta love time travel. The cause of and solution to all of life's problems.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoiler alert.
You have been warned.

The agent coming back into the past in and of itself prevented the event. Since the Foundation needed to contain him as an anomaly, and reports of the interview were available to all personnel of Clearance Level 3 and above, everyone in this iteration of the Foundation stayed on their toes for a full 90 days. Just that little bit extra precaution and lack of complacency kept a breach from occurring which otherwise would have killed millions of civilians and possibly compromised the secrecy of the Foundation.

Which SCP was it? Well, I don't know. It didn't happen. You'll have to ask B████…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No extranormal events of the scale described were recorded in the 90 days following this interview.

So did the agent coming back into the past, in and of itself, prevent that event, without the need for anyone to do anything? Or did he purposefully do some "butterfly flapping it's wing" type thing to prevent it? Or did he get lost when time travelling, and end up in a timeline where the event was never going to happen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, that's one hell of an article for a concept that is… simple. As simple as dealing with temporal anomalies while acting as a temporal anomaly can be, that is. The execution here is pretty darn good, explaining what the hell happens before throwing the bone for the final conclusion to be made. And that's good, because an SCP article should have a point of view that isn't in on the joke, so to speak.

I'm not sure what to make of document 1780-WL and the fact that it has such a hard link with this SCP, because I'm not really big on SCP's that work better with external resources. It's not that it's bad, just question of principle. And besides, I do like that document. It's a bit of a spoiler if you read it first, though. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ladies and gentlemen. We have *drumroll* a username drop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not to worry. I understand fortune actually favors the bold. ;3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
why doesn't the Foundation have a department for studying temporal anomalies?

If I understand everything correctly… they do, and it's SCP-1780-1. It acts as *the* Temporal Anomalies department, and part of its job is precisely to make its own existence unnecessary in every timeline, including its own.

Which, as I said somewhere else, is one hell of a bold goal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just make sure future-you remembers to come back :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll link past-me here so I can read this and upvote it o.o

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We don't see SCPs of this complexity often, and it really is quite a shame. It's been a real pleasure to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That reminds me: I am trying to assemble a canon revolving around this task force. If you have interest in time-travel plots, perhaps we can put heads together on this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True - "our" Foundation, for example, has some pretty strict rules about cross-experimenting with SCP objects (especially after some of the fiascos involving cross-testing of various items with 914 and 682). Using a temporal anomaly to contain another item, particularly a Keter, would, I would imagine, cause more than one Overseer to scream audibly before ordering all such testing to be suspended immediately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll go one further and say that most iterations of the Foundation don't have the personnel necessary to devote to the task, nor the will to use anomalous mechanisms to contain other, more dangerous anomalies. It takes a very particular type of intellectual and political climate for a department of temporal anomalies (much less one that actually employs them) to come about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It makes perfect sense though, just because you have clearance to read the Containment Procedures document doesn't mean that you can read the transcribed letter that says his name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps this Foundation simply doesn't know enough about how temporal anomalies work to make it a worthwhile venture. They might just end up having some people sit in a room and say "… yep, this is from the future.".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … y'know… why doesn't the Foundation have a department for studying temporal anomalies? We've got an entire underground city that was time-shifted from the future to when we found it. It even came with about fifty uncontained skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. Analysis of Office CB-████ has yielded no evidence of anomalous activity and may be safely inhabited.

This makes it sound like the analysis may be safely inhabited. Please fix this while I write an SCP that is a document that can be lived in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've gotten a lot of pretty high praise for this one, and I just wanted to say thank you all for helping make this SCiP and this character a thing. More is definitely forthcoming; hell, I haven't even told you all where he's from yet! Stay tuned!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, this is exactly like some of my dreams.

I wish I could +2, but I can only +1 :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Comprehend now my powers of prophecy! Last night, I said to you:

Mainlist that fucker.

See? Pretty much +50 in no time. This scip is the fukken shit. I like everything about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let me simply state: Awesome. Having just finished reading Iteration 0 and The Deep End, you're officially on my must read list. I will eagerly await them!
I'll admit that I'm getting a little bit of pride at finally picking up on some of these after ~a month on here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I love temporal anamolies and this is probably the best temporal-related creative work I've seen since Primer. (Nice cameo btw).

This is most excellent, and opens up an entire new avenue of Foundation canon. Great job, and I look forward to the skips and stories that this opens the door for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup, good eye. It's another really good retrocausal anomaly that poses a serious existential risk to civilization if it ever breaches containment. Got a few tales planned with VincentRedgrave wherein RCTF-Δt goes after them, figured I'd allude to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really really like this one. Referencing one of my favorite SCPs in a suitable subtle and logical way earns bonus points. The idea of the foundation having a "Time squad" is very well done.

One question though. is the first note a reference to scp-140?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I refer you to my post earlier on in this thread.

Also: yay! I got that one…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Incidentally, SCP-1968 ("the doughnut?!") makes a cameo in this piece :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thoughts after reading the first page: "My mind is full of fuck."

Thoughts after reading the second page: "My mind is full of EVEN MORE FUCK."

FFB, your SCPs can be a tad dense, but by God is it worth it to get through them in the end. +1, knowing that I'll need to reread it at some point in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Go read Iteration 0 and The Deep End for background on Thad Xyank

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ask yourself which one's the real Foundation: the one containing SCP-1780 or the one operating RCTF-Δt?

+Answer key
-Answer key

The correct answer is both and neither.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been trying to write this same concept (Foundation-future chrono-operations, Foundation-present being dense about it) for over a year, but could never get it to gel.

THANK YOU! for writing it so well and freeing me from that albatross.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like I'm missing the deeper implications of this article. Could someone explain it to me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is one long list of Oh shit.. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I rarely really feel the impetus to sit down and vote for an article.

I adore this skip.

Vaya con Xyank, mi amigo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is excellent in pretty much every capacity.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As the author of SCP-1968 Global Retrocausal Torus, I applaud the use of the word 'retrocausal'
Lovely SCP =D
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I can’t figure is why that answer is so controversial when the event in question will have happened one month ago.

And this is where I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was liking this, and then this popped up:

Who keeps using the doughnut?!

And then I was loving it. Upvoted with prejudice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No way. Making typos is like, my thing.

Also typing on a phone is hard ;~;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are they using this donut?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now, if Roget could just stop misspelling "dialogue"…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Part of the creepiness for me is that I actually used to know a guy named Greg Thompson, and the idea of him being a Foundation employee is… unsettling.
